heading: MODULUS TERTIUS DECIMUS
card_summary: This chunk opens the thirteenth module (Modulus Tertius Decimus) under the heading "Triumphus" (Triumph), with the first Concentus addressing the nature of a just battle ("Quae sit iusta pugna"). Zorzi applies the scholastic doctrine of just war to Christ's cosmic battle against the devil, arguing that the "ancient serpent" enslaved humanity by fraud and deceit, and that Christ's campaign against him satisfies all conditions of legitimate warfare: a just cause, formal declaration of war through the patriarchs and prophets acting as heralds (feciales), and authority granted by the Father. The scriptural witnesses cited include a Psalm on the liberation of the poor, John 12:31 (the prince of this world cast out), the temptation in the desert, and Isaiah's prophecy of the suffering servant. Zorzi concludes that Christ's victory was won not by fraud or vengeance but by truth against the liar, zeal against the envious, and goodness against the wicked.
essay:
## Overview: The Thirteenth Module and the Theology of Triumph

Chunk 125 introduces the *Modulus Tertius Decimus* under the thematic heading **Triumphus** (Triumph). The first *Concentus* poses a foundational jurisprudential question: *Quae sit iusta pugna* — what constitutes a just battle. Zorzi's strategy is to apply the classical and scholastic criteria for *bellum iustum* (just war) to the cosmic, soteriological struggle between Christ and the devil, demonstrating that the Redemption was not only spiritually but legally and rationally perfect.

## The Criteria of Just War Applied to the Redemption

Zorzi opens with a formal statement of the requirements for a noble and glorious triumph:

> Nobilis triumphus et gloriosus exquirit primo aequam pugnam, et pulchram victoriam. Et tunc pugna aequa est, quando adest legitima ratio pugnandi, bello etiam prius indicto, principis item authoritate interveniente, cuius est decernere bellum, et quod iure pugnetur, non autem malae ultionis causa.

<cite>p. 2670</cite>

The conditions enumerated — legitimate reason for fighting, prior formal declaration of war, authority of the sovereign, and the exclusion of mere revenge — mirror the standard criteria of just war theory as developed from Cicero through Augustine and Aquinas. Zorzi's application of this legal-theological framework to the Incarnation and Passion is characteristic of his approach throughout *De Harmonia Mundi*: every dimension of sacred history is shown to conform to the highest standards of reason, beauty, and order.

## Christ as Emperor and the Tyranny of the Devil

Zorzi names Christ "Imperator noster" — our Emperor — a title that places the Redemption within an imperial, triumphalist framework consonant with the *Tono Ottavo* heading under which this section falls (as indicated at p. 2503 in the editorial marker). The adversary is identified as "antiquus ille serpens, qui est diabolus," the ancient serpent who is the devil, borrowing the Apocalyptic title from Revelation 12:9 and 20:2. Zorzi characterizes the devil's dominion as:

> tamquam praedo immanissimus antiquus ille serpens, qui est diabolus, detineret ipsum humanum genus sub durissimo, horridoque iugo servitutis, quod quidem dolo, et fraude multiplici subiecerat.

<cite>p. 2670</cite>

The devil's rule is defined by its illegitimacy: it was obtained by "dolo et fraude multiplici" — by manifold deceit and fraud, alluding to the temptation of Adam and Eve. This establishes the justice of Christ's counter-campaign: the liberation of an unjustly enslaved people constitutes a paradigmatic just cause.

## The Feciales: Patriarchs and Prophets as Formal Heralds

One of the most legally sophisticated moves in the passage is Zorzi's invocation of the *feciales*, the Roman priestly college responsible for the formal declaration of war. He argues that the divine war against the devil was properly declared in advance through the patriarchs and prophets, who served in the capacity of feciales:

> indictum est ei bellum per antiquos patres, prophetasque, quibus veluti fecialibus hoc fuerat demandatum.

<cite>p. 2670</cite>

This is a remarkable piece of theological jurisprudence: the entire prophetic tradition of Israel is reimagined as the ceremonial declaration of eschatological war, ensuring that even the enemy (the devil) could not claim to have been ambushed or treated unjustly.

## Scriptural Witnesses

Zorzi marshals several scriptural passages as evidence:

1. **The Psalm on liberation of the poor**: "Liberavit pauperem a potente, et pauperem, cui non erat adiutor" — a citation from the Psalms (likely Ps. 71/72:12) where the "royal prophet" (David) is described as the "most solemn herald of God" (*solennissimus praeco Dei*). The poor man enslaved to a powerful oppressor is the typological figure for humanity under the devil.

2. **John 12:31**: "Nunc princeps huius mundi eiicietur foras" — Christ's own public declaration that the prince of this world would be cast out, treated here as the Prince's formal proclamation of intent before the engagement.

3. **The Temptation in the Desert**: Zorzi notes that the enemy himself initiated hostilities when he attacked Christ during his fast in the desert, further validating the justice of Christ's response.

4. **Isaiah 53:8 (paraphrased)**: The Father's authority for the war is grounded in Isaiah's prophecy: "Propter peccata populi mei percussi eum, id est tradidi percutiendum, et vulnerandum" — "On account of the sins of my people I struck him, that is, I delivered him to be struck and wounded." This reframes the Passion as an act of sovereign authority (*auctoritas bellandi*) granted by the Father.

## Victory Through Wounds: The Paradox of the New Mode of Battle

Zorzi introduces a striking paradox near the end of the passage:

> Nam in hoc novo genere pugnae victoria reportanda erat vulneribus

<cite>p. 2671</cite>

In this "new kind of battle," victory was to be won through wounds — a paradox central to the theology of the Cross. The inversion of conventional martial logic (where wounds signal defeat) is presented as the defining characteristic of Christ's campaign. This "new genus of battle" (*novum genus pugnae*) is free from all fraud or desire for revenge; it is animated entirely by the *pietas* of liberating the unjustly oppressed.

## The Antithetical Climax

The passage closes with a rhetorical triplet that structures Christ's virtues against the devil's vices:

> Pugnavit itaque veritate contra mendacem hostem, pugnavit zelo contra invidum, bonitate contra malivolum, et scelestum.

<cite>p. 2671</cite>

Truth against the liar; zeal against the envious; goodness against the wicked and malevolent. This antithetical construction is both rhetorically elegant and theologically precise, defining Christ's triumph as the enactment of divine attributes in direct opposition to the defining vices of Satan.

## Connections to Zorzi's Larger Project

This section belongs to the culminating movement of *De Harmonia Mundi*, where cosmic harmony is demonstrated not only through number, music, and Neoplatonic emanation but through the historical drama of salvation. The application of Roman legal categories (*feciales*, *auctoritas*, *iusta causa*) to the Incarnation reflects Zorzi's characteristic synthesis of classical learning and Christian theology. The imperial title "Imperator noster Christus" echoes the triumphal vocabulary of the whole module (*Triumphus*) and points toward the eschatological resolution of the cosmic war — the final harmony achieved through the paradoxical victory of suffering love.
